elements.sent-2411-001: The Tallis-Leyton model is a simple model of parasite acquisition where there is no interaction between the host and the acquired parasites.
elements.sent-2411-002: We examine the effect of model parameters on the distribution of the host's parasite burden in the sense of the Lorenz order.
elements.sent-2411-003: This fits with an alternate view of parasite aggregation that has become widely used in empirical studies but is rarely used in the analysis of mathematical models of parasite acquisition.
elements.sent-2411-004: The distribution of parasites among its host population typically displays a high degree of inequality.
elements.sent-2411-005: In fact, this phenomenon, called aggregation, is almost a universally observed \citep{SD:95, Poulin:07 .}
elements.sent-2411-006: Despite its importance, aggregation lacks a universally accepted definition.
elements.sent-2411-007: Instead, the phenomenon is studied using a number of common measures of aggregation that summarise different properties of the parasite's distribution \citep{Pielou:77,ML:2020, MPF:2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-008: The most commonly used measures of aggregation in theoretical models are the $k$ parameter of the negative binomial distribution \citep{AM:78a, AM:78b, RP:02, Schreiber:06, McPherson:12 and the variance-to-mean ratio \citep{Isham:95, BP:00, HI:2000, Peacock:18 .}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Both of these measures can be interpreted as quantifying how far the distribution of parasites deviates from a Poisson distribution.
elements.sent-2411-010: An alternative view of aggregation was put forward by \citet{Poulin:1993 , arguing that a measure of the discrepancy between the observed distribution of parasites in the hosts and the ideal distributions where all hosts are infected with the same number of parasites would be the best measure of aggregation.}
elements.sent-2411-011: This view puts the Lorenz ordering of distributions \citep{Lorenz:1905,Arnold:87 central in the study of aggregation.}
elements.sent-2411-012: As a measure of this discrepancy, Poulin proposed his index, {\em D , which is essentially an estimator of the Gini index \citep{Gini:24 .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: This has since become become one of the standard measure of aggregation used in studies of wild parasite populations.
elements.sent-2411-014: Although the Gini index has been commonly used in empirical studies since Poulin's proposal, we are unaware of any examination of its behaviour in theoretical models of parasite acquisition.
elements.sent-2411-015: The aim of this paper is to apply Poulin's view of aggregation to a simple stochastic model for the number of parasites in a definitive host proposed by \citet{TL:1969 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: The Tallis-Leyton model assumes that the host makes infective contacts following a Poisson process and at each infective contact a random number of parasites enter the host.
elements.sent-2411-017: Once a parasite enters the host, it is alive for a random period of time.
elements.sent-2411-018: The lifetimes of parasites, numbers of parasites entering the host at infective contacts, and the Poisson process of infective contacts are all assumed to be independent.
elements.sent-2411-019: Furthermore, the parasites are assumed to have no effect on the host mortality.
elements.sent-2411-020: In Section 2 we review some background on the Lorenz ordering and the closely related convex ordering of distributions.
elements.sent-2411-021: The Tallis-Leyton model is analysed in Section 3.
elements.sent-2411-022: We first show show how the host's parasite burden can be represented as a compound Poisson distribution.
elements.sent-2411-023: This representation is then applied to determine how each of the model parameters affect the Lorenz ordering of the distribution of parasites in the host.
elements.sent-2411-024: The final part of the analysis shows that the host's parasite burden is asymptotically normally distributed in the limit as the rate of infectious contacts goes to infinity.
elements.sent-2411-025: The paper concludes with a discussion of future challenges in analysing models of parasite aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-026: The host is assumed to be parasite free at birth so $M(0) = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-027: The host makes infective contacts during its lifetime following a Poisson process with rate parameter $\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-028: At each infective contact, the number of parasites that enter the host is a random variable $N$ and once a parasite enters the host it survives for a random period $T$.
elements.sent-2411-029: The parasites have no effect on the host mortality.
elements.sent-2411-030: Although we won't make use of this fact, we note that this process also describes an infinite server queue with bulk arrivals and general independent service times \citep{HCK:83 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: We let $G_X$, $F_X$ and $\bar{F _X = 1 - F_{X}$ denote the probability generating function (PGF), distribution function, and survival function of a random variable $X$.}
elements.sent-2411-032: We write $G_{M (\cdot;a)$ for the PGF of $ M(a)$, conditional on survival of the host to age $ a$.. \citet{TL:1969 showed \begin{equation}G_{M}(z;a) = \exp \left(\lambda \int_{0}^{a} \left[G_{N}(1 + \bar{F}_{T}(a-s) (z-1)) -1 \right] ds\right).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Standard arguments show that the mean and variance of $M(a)$ are \begin{equation}\mu(a) = \lambda E N \int_{0}^{a} \bar{F}_{T}(s)\, ds \sigma^{2}(a) = \lambda E N(N-1) \int_{0}^{a} \bar{F}_{T}^2(s)\, ds + \lambda E N \int_{0}^{a} \bar{F}_{T}(s)\, ds.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\mu(a) = \lambda E N \int_{0}^{a} \bar{F}_{T}(s)\, ds \sigma^{2}(a) = \lambda E N(N-1) \int_{0}^{a} \bar{F}_{T}^2(s)\, ds + \lambda E N \int_{0}^{a} \bar{F}_{T}(s)\, ds.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-034: Hence, the variance-to-mean ratio is \begin{equation}\label{eq:TL_VMR} \text{VMR}(M(a)) = 1 + \frac{\mathbb{E} N(N-1)}{\mathbb{E} N} \frac{\int^a_0 \bar{F}^{2}_{T}(s) ds}{\int^a_0 \bar{F}_{T}(s) ds}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-035: Assuming $\mathbb{E N < \infty$ and $\mathbb{E T < \infty $, the limiting distribution of parasite burden as $ a\to\infty$ exists and has PGF \begin{equation}G_{M}(z,\infty) = \exp \left(\lambda \int_{0}^{\infty} \left[G_{N}(1 + \bar{F}_{T}(s) (z-1)) -1 \right] ds\right).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Since scaling of the host age, the lifetime distribution of parasites, and the inverse of the rate of infective contacts by a common factor results in the same distribution for the host's parasite burden, we assume that the expected lifetime of a parasite is 1.
elements.sent-2411-037: The following general definition of the Lorenz curve was given by \citet{Gastwirth:1971 .}
elements.sent-2411-038: The Lorenz curve $L: [0,1] \to [0,1] $ for the distribution $F$ with finite mean $ \mu$ is given by \begin{equation}L(u) = \frac{\int_{0}^{u} F^{-1}(y)\, dy}{\mu},\end{equation} where $ F^{-1}$ is the quantile function \begin{equation}F^{-1}(y) = \sup \{ x : F(x) \leq y \} \quad for  y \in (0,1).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-039: Adapting the description in \citet[Section 3.1]{Arnold:87 to a parasitology context, the Lorenz curve $L(u)$ represents the proportion of the parasite population infecting the least infected $u$ proportion of the host population.}
elements.sent-2411-040: When all hosts are infected with the same number of parasite, the Lorenz curve is given by $L(u) = u$ and is called the egalitarian line.
elements.sent-2411-041: Note that $L_{X (u) \geq u$ for all $ u \in [0,1]$.}
elements.sent-2411-042: The Lorenz curve defines a partial order on the class of all distributions on $[0,\infty)$ with finite mean \citep[Definition 3.2.1]{Arnold:87 .}
elements.sent-2411-043: Let $ X$ and $Y$ be random variables with the respective Lorenz curves denoted $ L_{X $ and $ L_{Y}$.}
elements.sent-2411-044: We say $ X$ is smaller in the Lorenz order, denoted $ X \leq_{\rm{Lorenz Y $ if $ L_{X (u) \geq L_{Y (u)$ for every $ u \in [0,1]$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: The Lorenz curves of some standard distributions are given in \citet[Section 6.1]{Arnold:87 .}
elements.sent-2411-046: The negative binomial distribution is extensively used in parasitology.
elements.sent-2411-047: When negative binomial distributions are parameterised in terms of the mean $m$ and $k$, they can be compared in the Lorenz order \citep{ML:2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-048: Specifically, let $ \mathsf{NB (m,k)$ denote the negative binomial distribution with PGF \begin{equation}G(z) = \left(\frac{k}{k + m - mz} \right)^{k}.\end{equation}. Then.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Closely related to the Lorenz order is the convex order of distributions.
elements.sent-2411-050: Let $X$ and $Y$ be two random variables such that $\mathbb{E X = \mathbb{E Y$.}}
elements.sent-2411-051: We say $X$ is small than $Y$ in the convex order, denoted $X \leq_{\rm cx Y$, if $ \mathbb{E \phi(X) \leq \mathbb{E \phi(Y)$ for all convex functions $ \phi: \mathbb{R \to \mathbb{R}$, provided the expectations exist.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: These two orderings are related since $ X \leq_{\rm Lorenz Y$ if and only if \begin{equation}E\, \phi\left(\frac{X}{EX}\right) \leq E\, \phi\left(\frac{Y}{EY}\right)\end{equation} for every continuous convex function $\phi$ \citep[Corollary 3.2.1]{Arnold:87 .}}
elements.sent-2411-053: In other words, \begin{equation}X \leq_{\rm Lorenz} Y is equivalent to \frac{X}{E X} \leq_{\rm cx} \frac{Y}{E Y}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation}X \leq_{\rm Lorenz} Y is equivalent to \frac{X}{E X} \leq_{\rm cx} \frac{Y}{E Y}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-054: We briefly mention some of the important results that are used in our analysis.
elements.sent-2411-055: The convex order is {\em closed under weak limits provided the expectations also converge \citep[Theorem 3.A.12 (c)]{SS:07 .}}
elements.sent-2411-056: The convex order is {\em closed under mixtures \citep[Theorem 3.A.12 (b)]{SS:07 .}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Let $X$, $Y$, and $\Theta$ be random variables and write $[X \mid \Theta = \theta] $ and $ [Y \mid \Theta = \theta]$ for the conditional distributions of $X$ and $Y$ given $ \Theta = \theta$.
elements.sent-2411-058: If $ [X \mid \Theta = \theta] \leq_{\rm cx [Y \mid \Theta = \theta]$ for all $\theta$ in the support of $\Theta$, then $X \leq_{\rm cx Y$.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: As an application of this property we can say that if $X \leq_{\rm cx Y$ and $Z$ is an independent non-negative random variable, then $ ZX \leq_{\rm cx ZY$.}}
elements.sent-2411-060: The convex order is {\em closed under convolutions \citep[Theorem 3.A.12 (d)]{SS:07 .}}
elements.sent-2411-061: Let $X_1, X_2,\ldots, X_k$ and $Y_1, Y_2, \ldots, Y_k$ be two sets of independent random variables .
elements.sent-2411-062: If $X_j \leq_{\rm cx Y_{j}$ for $j=1,2,\ldots,k$, then \begin{equation}\sum_{j=1}^{k} X_{j} \leq_{\rm cx} \sum_{j=1}^{k} Y_{j}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Combining the properties of closure under mixtures and closure under convolutions, we see the convex order is {\em closed under random sums so \begin{equation}\sum_{j=1}^{K} X_{j} \leq_{\rm cx} \sum_{j=1}^{K} Y_{j},\end{equation} for any non-negative integer random variable $K$.}
elements.sent-2411-064: As an application of the closure under random sums property of the convex order, consider two random variables $K$ and $\tilde{K}$ that related by binomial thinning.
elements.sent-2411-065: That is, $G_{\tilde{K (z) = G_{K (1 - p + pz)$ for some $p \in (0,1)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: The closure under random sums property can be adapted to the case where the $X_1, X_2, \ldots$ and $Y_1, Y_2, \ldots$ are two iid sequences with $X \leq_{\rm cx Y$, and $K_1$ and $K_2$ are non-negative integer random variables such that $K_{1 \leq_{\rm cx K_{2}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: In this case, \citet[Theorem 3.A.13]{SS:07 implies \begin{equation}\sum_{j=1}^{K_1} X_{j} \leq_{\rm cx} \sum_{j=1}^{K_2} Y_{j}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-069: The survival function can be used to establish if two random variables can be compared in the convex order.
elements.sent-2411-070: If $X$ and $Y$ are two random variables with the same mean, then $X \leq_{\rm cx Y$ if $\bar{F _X - \bar{F _Y$ has a single sign change and the sign sequence is $ +, -$ \citep[Theorem 3.A.44(b)]{SS:07 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: This property can also be used to characterise the convex order \citep[Theorem 3.A.45]{SS:07 .}
elements.sent-2411-072: In practice, levels of aggregation are compared with numerical summaries rather than using the entire Lorenz curve.
elements.sent-2411-073: A useful measure of aggregation respects the Lorenz ordering.
elements.sent-2411-074: That is, if $I(\cdot)$ is a measure of aggregation that respects the Lorenz ordering and $X \leq_{\rm Lorenz Y$, then $I(X) \leq I(Y)$.}
elements.sent-2411-075: We restrict our attention in this paper to the Gini index, the Hoover index (also known as the Pietra index, or the Robin-Hood index), $ 1 - \text{prevalence $, and the coefficient of variation.}
elements.sent-2411-076: The Gini index \citep{Gini:24 is given by twice the area between the egalitarian line and the Lorenz curve.}
elements.sent-2411-077: For a random variable $X$, the Gini index can be expressed as \begin{equation}G(X) = \frac{E\, |X - \tilde{X}|}{2\, E X},\end{equation} where $ \tilde{X}$ is an independent random variable with $ \tilde{X \stackrel{d {= X $.}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: The Hoover index is given by the maximum vertical distance between the egalitarian line and the Lorenz curve.
elements.sent-2411-079: The Hoover index can be expressed as \begin{equation}H(X) = \frac{E\left|X - E X\right|}{2\, E X}\end{equation} Prevalence, the probability that a host is infected by at least one parasite, is an important quantity in parasitology.
elements.sent-2411-080: Although prevalence is not usually thought of as a measure of aggregation, we may express $ 1 - \text{prevalence $ in terms of the Lorenz curve as \begin{equation}1 - prevalence = \max\{u: L(u) = 0 \}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-081: For the Tallis-Leyton model, \begin{equation}1 - \text{prevalence} = G_{M}(0;a) = \exp \left(\lambda \int_{0}^{a} \left[G_{N}(1 - \bar{F}_{T}(a-s)) -1 \right] ds\right). \label{eq:1minusPrev}\end{equation} Finally, the coefficient of variation is given by \begin{equation}CV(X) = \frac{\sqrt{Var(X)}}{E X}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-082: This measure is rarely used in parasitology, though it is mentioned in some reviews on parasite aggregation such as \citet{Wilson:2001 and \citet{ML:2020 .}}
elements.sent-2411-083: As means and variances are commonly reported in empirical studies and are often easily calculated for theoretical models, it may be useful in some contexts.
elements.sent-2411-084: For example, the squared coefficient of variation for the Tallis-Leyton model is \begin{equation}\label{eq:TL_CV} \text{CV}^{2}(M(a)) = \frac{1}{\lambda} \frac{\mathbb{E} N(N-1)}{(\mathbb{E} N)^{2}} \frac{\int^a_0 \bar{F}^{2}_{T}(s) ds}{ \left( \int^a_0 \bar{F}_{T}(s) ds \right)^{2}} + \frac{1}{\lambda \mathbb{E} N \int^a_0 \bar{F}_{T}(s) ds}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-018: \begin{equation}\label{eq:TL_CV} CV^{2}(M(a)) = \frac{1}{\lambda} \frac{E N(N-1)}{(E N)^{2}} \frac{\int^a_0 \bar{F}^{2}_{T}(s) ds}{ \left( \int^a_0 \bar{F}_{T}(s) ds \right)^{2}} + \frac{1}{\lambda E N \int^a_0 \bar{F}_{T}(s) ds}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-085: These indices are related by the following inequality \begin{equation}1 - prevalence \leq H(X) \leq G(X) \leq H(X) (2- H(X)) \leq CV(X)\end{equation} \citep{Taguchi:68, ML:2020 .}
elements.formula-2411-019: \begin{equation}1 - prevalence \leq H(X) \leq G(X) \leq H(X) (2- H(X)) \leq CV(X)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-086: In particular, if $ \mathbb{E X \leq 1$, then we have the equality $1 - \text{prevalence = H(X)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-087: The Gini index and Hoover index can be further related to the coefficient of variation when the distribution of parasites is approximately normal.

elements.sent-2411-101: The analysis begins with a representation of the host's parasite burden as a compound Poisson distribution.
elements.sent-2411-102: This representation is used extensively to understand how the rate of infective contacts, the distribution of the number of parasites that enter the host during an infective contact, the age of the host, and lifetime distribution of the parasites all affect the distribution of parasites in the host in terms of the Lorenz order.

elements.sent-2411-220: From the expressions for $R_N $ and $\mu(a)$, \begin{equation}\lambda \int^a_0 \left[ G_{N}(1 + \bar{F}_{T}(a-s) (e^{i \tfrac{\omega}{\sigma(a)}}-1) ) - 1 \right] ds - i \tfrac{\omega \mu(a)}{\sigma(a)} = \lambda G_{N}'(1) \left(\int^a_0 \bar{F}_{T}(a-s) ds\right) R_{1} \left(\frac{\omega}{\sigma(a)} \right) + \frac{\lambda}{2} G_{N}''(1) \left(\int^a_0\bar{F}_{T}^2(a-s) ds\right) R_{0} \left(\frac{\omega}{\sigma(a)} \right)^2 + \lambda \int_0^a R_{N}\left(\bar{F}_{T}(a-s) (e^{i\tfrac{\omega}{\sigma(a)}} -1 ) \right) ds\end{equation} From the expression for $\sigma^{2 (a)$ and the fact that \begin{equation}R_{1}(x)^2 + x^{2} = R_2(2x) - 2R_2(x),\end{equation} we obtain \begin{equation}\lambda \int^a_0 \left[ G_{N}(1 + \bar{F}_{T}(a-s) (e^{i \tfrac{\omega}{\sigma(a)}}-1) ) - 1 \right] ds - i \tfrac{\omega \mu(a)}{\sigma(a)} = -\frac{\omega^{2}}{2} + \lambda G_{N}'(1) \left(\int^a_0 \bar{F}_{T}(a-s) ds\right) R_{2} \left(\frac{\omega}{\sigma(a)} \right) + \frac{\lambda}{2} G_{N}''(1) \left(\int^a_0\bar{F}_{T}^2(a-s) ds\right) \left( R_{2} \left(\frac{2\omega}{\sigma(a)} \right) - 2 R_{2} \left(\frac{\omega}{\sigma(a)} \right) \right) + \lambda \int_0^a R_{N}\left(\bar{F}_{T}(a-s) (e^{i\tfrac{\omega}{\sigma(a)}} -1 ) \right) ds\end{equation} Using the bound (\ref{eq:ANbound ) and the fact that $\sigma^{2 (a) \propto \lambda $, we see \begin{equation}\lim_{\lambda\to\infty} \lambda G_{N}'(1) \left(\int^a_0 \bar{F}_{T}(a-s) ds\right) R_{2} \left(\frac{\omega}{\sigma(a)} \right) = 0\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\lim_{\lambda\to\infty} \frac{\lambda}{2} G_{N}''(1) \left(\int^a_0\bar{F}_{T}^2(a-s) ds\right) \left( R_{2} \left(\frac{2\omega}{\sigma(a)} \right) - 2 R_{2} \left(\frac{\omega}{\sigma(a)} \right) \right) = 0.\end{equation}.}}}

elements.sent-2411-230: In this paper, we examined the aggregation of a host's parasite burden in the Tallis-Leyton model, interpreting aggregation in terms of the Lorenz order.
elements.sent-2411-231: Our analysis showed that increasing the host's age or the rate of infective contacts decreases aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-232: Similarly, increasing the aggregation of the clumps of parasites that enter the host as infective contacts also increases the aggregation of the host's parasite burden.
elements.sent-2411-233: On the other hand, less variability in the parasite's lifetime distribution, as defined in the convex order, results in greater aggregation of the host's parasite burden.
elements.sent-2411-234: Unfortunately, the population dynamics of parasites are often more complicated than what is represented in the Tallis-Leyton model.
elements.sent-2411-235: Some parasites need multiple hosts to complete its life cycle.
elements.sent-2411-236: Once a parasite finds a host it may be subject to intraspecific and interspecific competition for resources.
elements.sent-2411-237: Furthermore, parasites often interact with the host either by stimulating an immune response from the host or by increasing the host's mortality rate.
elements.sent-2411-238: In Isham's model, the host acquires parasites following the same dynamics as the Tallis-Leyton model and parasite lifetimes are assumed exponentially distributed.
elements.sent-2411-239: The important difference in Isham's model is that each parasite present in the host increases the host's death rate by a fixed amount $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-240: A complete analysis of Isham's model in terms of the Lorenz order is beyond the scope of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-241: In a special case, however, we can see that parasite induced host mortality increases aggregation of the parasite distribution, as interpreted in the Lorenz order.
elements.sent-2411-242: When the number of parasites that enter the host at an infective contact follows a geometric distribution, an explicit expression for the limiting distribution is possible.
elements.sent-2411-243: Specifically, if $ N \sim \mathsf{NB (m,1)$, then \begin{equation}M(\infty) \sim NB\left(\frac{\lambda m}{ E T + \alpha + \alpha m} , \frac{\lambda}{ E T + \alpha + \alpha m}\right).\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-063: \begin{equation}M(\infty) \sim NB\left(\frac{\lambda m}{ E T + \alpha + \alpha m} , \frac{\lambda}{ E T + \alpha + \alpha m}\right).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-244: As the negative binomial distribution is decreasing in Lorenz order in both mean and $k$, it follows that indices respecting the Lorenz order are increasing in the parasite induced host mortality rate.
elements.sent-2411-245: In contrast, the variance-to-mean ratio is $1 + m $ so it is not affected by the parasite induced mortality.
elements.sent-2411-246: A complete examination Isham's model in terms of the Lorenz order may prove challenging.
elements.sent-2411-247: Even computing the Gini and Hoover indices may present difficulties since they require absolute moments, which are often not easily evaluated.
elements.sent-2411-248: In that case, the coefficient of variation may prove useful since it respects the Lorenz order, is easily evaluated, and can be used to approximate the Gini and Hoover indices when the distribution is approximately normal.
